1: A shadow: “The word of the LORD came to Jonah a second time.” The second commissioning — after failure, discipline, and rescue — is the OT pattern of divine recommissioning that reaches its fullest form in the resurrection appearances. The disciples who scattered at the arrest (Mark 14:50) and Peter who denied (John 18:17, 25-27) are recommissioned at the resurrection and at Pentecost: “Go and make disciples of all nations” (Matthew 28:19). YHWH does not abandon his messengers after their failure; he restores and re-sends. The God of the second call is the God whose purposes cannot be finally thwarted by human disobedience.
2: A revelation of God: “Get up and go to Nineveh, that great city, and proclaim to it the message I give you.” The command is unchanged from the first calling (Jonah 1:2); YHWH's purpose for Nineveh has not been altered by Jonah's flight. This reveals the perseverance of divine purpose — what YHWH intends to accomplish will not be prevented by human failures or delays. Christ's mission is characterized by this same perseverance: “I will build my church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it” (Matthew 16:18). The gospel proclaimed to Nineveh cannot be stopped by a fleeing prophet; the gospel of Christ cannot be stopped by crucifixion or persecution.
3: A theme: “Jonah went to Nineveh, just as the LORD had commanded.” Obedience after discipline — full, unconditioned compliance with the divine command. The “just as the LORD had commanded” formula marks complete obedience. Christ's obedience is not disciplined obedience (corrected after failure) but perfect obedience from the start: “I always do what pleases him” (John 8:29); “obedient even to death, even death on a cross” (Philippians 2:8). Jonah's finally-achieved obedience is a pale shadow of the obedience that characterizes the Son — but it demonstrates that the mission requires genuine human obedience, which only Christ perfectly embodies.
4: A shadow: Jonah's preaching to Nineveh — just five Hebrew words in the original (“Yet forty days, and Nineveh shall be overthrown!”) — produces the greatest recorded revival in the OT. The minimal content of the message contrasts with the maximal effectiveness of the response. Christ says of John the Baptist: “Among those born of women there has arisen no one greater than John” — and yet “something greater than Jonah is here” (Matthew 12:41). If Jonah's spare announcement of judgment produced mass repentance, how much more the gospel of Christ's death and resurrection? The power is in the divine word, not in the messenger's eloquence or the message's length.
5: A shadow: “The people of Nineveh believed God.” Mass Gentile repentance in response to a Jewish prophet's preaching — the entire city, from greatest to least, responds to the word of YHWH. This anticipates the Gentile response to the gospel throughout Acts: Cornelius's household (Acts 10:44-48), the Greeks who “believed and turned to the Lord” (Acts 11:21), the “God-fearers” who respond more readily than the synagogue (Acts 13:48: “as many as were appointed to eternal life believed”). Jesus explicitly cites Nineveh's response as the standard against which his own generation is judged (Matthew 12:41): Gentiles repented at the preaching of Jonah; this generation rejects something greater.
6: A shadow: the king of Nineveh descends from his throne, removes his robe, puts on sackcloth, and sits in ashes — a great king's voluntary humiliation before the word of YHWH. The voluntary descent from throne and honor is the structural shape of the incarnation: “though he was in the form of God, he did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself, taking the form of a servant” (Philippians 2:6-7). The Ninevite king's humiliation is compelled by fear of judgment; Christ's descent is motivated by love and accomplished through obedience. The shape is the same; the motive and the agent are incomparably different.
7: A theme: the royal decree of fasting and repentance — extending even to the animals — expresses the comprehensiveness of the response required. When YHWH's word reaches a city, it claims total allegiance, not selective compliance. The NT equivalent is the call to complete, wholehearted discipleship: “Love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind” (Matthew 22:37). Christ does not come to improve selective aspects of life but to claim the whole person — and the whole creation, which “waits with eager longing for the revealing of the sons of God” (Romans 8:19), is caught up in the redemption he accomplishes.
8: A revelation of God: “Who knows? God may turn and relent and turn from his fierce anger, so that we may not perish.” The Ninevite king's prayer expresses the heart of the OT's doctrine of repentance and divine responsiveness: YHWH is not mechanically determined to destroy; genuine repentance opens the possibility of mercy. This is the character YHWH himself announces in Exodus 34:6-7 and Jonah confirms (Jonah 4:2). Christ's death is the ground on which YHWH can relent not merely for Nineveh but for the whole world: the penalty falls on Christ, so that those who repent find mercy rather than judgment (Acts 3:19: “Repent therefore, and turn back, that your sins may be blotted out”).
9: A revelation of God: “When God saw what they did and how they turned from their evil ways, he relented and did not bring on them the disaster he had threatened.” Divine responsiveness to genuine human repentance — YHWH does what the king hoped he might (v9): he relents. This reveals the God who does not take pleasure in the death of the wicked (Ezekiel 18:23: “Have I any pleasure in the death of the wicked, declares the Lord GOD, and not rather that he should turn from his way and live?”). Christ's atonement makes this responsiveness permanently and perfectly available: those who repent find not a God who “may” relent (v9's “who knows?”) but one who has already acted in Christ to make full forgiveness possible.
10: A revelation of God: YHWH relents from the disaster he had announced — his mercy triumphs over judgment (James 2:13). The theological issue this creates (how can a God who announces judgment not follow through?) is the theodicy Jonah grapples with in Jonah 4 and that Paul addresses in Romans: “Is God unjust? Not at all!” (Romans 9:14). The answer the NT provides is the cross: the judgment Nineveh escaped in Jonah 3 does not simply disappear — it is borne by Christ (Romans 3:25-26: “God presented Christ as a sacrifice of atonement... he did it to demonstrate his justice”). YHWH's relenting toward Nineveh is the shadow; Christ bearing the judgment so YHWH can justly relent toward all who repent is the substance.
